cards.explanation:
Chapter 20 covered advanced function topics:
  • Function pointers - storing and passing functions
  • Stack vs heap memory management
  • Recursion - functions that call themselves
  • Command line arguments (argc, argv)
  • Ellipsis and why to avoid them
  • Lambda expressions (anonymous functions)
  • Lambda captures (accessing outer scope)
examples: auto lambda = [x](int y) { return x + y; };

cards.front: What is the modern C++ way to store function pointers?
cards.back: Use std::function<returnType(params)> from <functional> header, or auto for lambda storage. This is cleaner and safer than raw function pointer syntax.

cards.front: What are the key differences between stack and heap memory?
cards.back:
Stack: Fast, automatic, limited size, LIFO order
Heap: Slower, manual management, large size, flexible lifetime
Use stack by default, heap when needed for large/dynamic allocations.

cards.text: Every recursive function needs a {{c1::base case}} to terminate and a {{c2::recursive case}} that moves toward the base case. Without proper base case, you get {{c3::stack overflow}}.
Command line arguments: {{c1::argc}} is the count (including program name), {{c2::argv}} is the array of C-style strings, with {{c3::argv[0]}} being the program name.

cards.front: Why should you avoid ellipsis (...) in modern C++?
cards.back: Ellipsis has no type safety, requires manual parsing with va_list macros, and can cause undefined behavior. Use variadic templates, std::initializer_list, or function overloading instead.

cards.text: Lambda syntax: {{c1::[captures]}}(parameters) {{c2::-> returnType}} { body }. The capture clause and parameter list are required, return type is {{c3::optional}}.

cards.front: What's the difference between [=] and [&] lambda captures?
cards.back:
[=] captures all used variables by value (makes copies)
[&] captures all used variables by reference (can modify originals, but risky if lambda outlives variables)

cards.concept: When to use each function technique
cards.explanation:
Choose the right tool:
  • Regular functions: Reusable, complex logic, multiple uses
  • Function pointers/std::function: Callbacks, storing functions, runtime selection
  • Lambdas: Short, one-off operations, closures, STL algorithms
  • Recursion: Tree/graph traversal, divide-and-conquer, natural subproblems
examples: sort(v.begin(), v.end(), [](int a, int b){ return a < b; }); // Lambda
std::function<int(int)> ops[] = {add, sub, mul}; // Function storage

cards.front: What are the main benefits of lambdas over traditional function pointers?
cards.back:
• Cleaner syntax, defined at point of use
  • Can capture local variables (closures)
  • Compiler can inline more easily
  • Type-safe with auto deduction
  • No need to name simple functions
